Josh: 	Okay, alright. So that was that one. David wants to know if you can

Jon: 	Okay, I will briefly cover that; but just so you know, that is all in your
USP videos online. But let’s cover that, briefly.
Your prime benefit, the very first exercise we had you do is go through
and determine what your USP is. We called that exercise the USP
Factory. That’s your unique selling position. That you want to find out
what makes your business unique.
And the question we ask is what are the top 3 problems your business
solves? And those became your top 3 benefits; or your product solves.
So, your primary benefit is the number one thing, that one thing that
stands out above all else, that makes you the most unique. This is the
one that exceeds your competition, usually, or the one that your
business is strongest at. That’s your primary benefit.
And then the driving force is the reason that primary benefit works.
Let me give you the example of mine. My primary benefit with my
Every Other Day Diet book is – this is my USP – the first diet ever
created to allow you to eat your favorite foods and still lose weight. So
that’s the whole USP. That’s taken from my primary benefit, which is
eat your favorite foods while losing weight. Almost wrote my whole
USP with the primary benefit. So, that’s a unique thing.
Then, the driving force is how does that work? What mechanism
allows you to eat your favorite foods and still lose weight? And how
that works is a process called caloric staggering and thermogenesis.
So that’s the drivers, that’s the primary, that’s the driving force behind
why that works.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

So, that’s going to be, “I’m going to show you exactly how you can eat

that is the driving force. The driving force is caloric and macronutrient

4
to stay away from is some guy that’s like, “Hi, my name is Bob, and I
want to,” that kind of radio kind of voice that sounds completely fake.
No. I’ve heard a couple of video sales letters like that, and they were
just a joke.
It’s like Shakeway. It’s just something people do for a gag, I think.
But if you get a guy that sounds like a real voice, he can have a good
voice, but just seems to be real and have a lot of reality to him. Then,
he can be your ghostwriter. There’s no difference in that than a
ghostwriter.
For example, when you see a book: fill in the blank president with fill in
the blank ghostwriter, the ghostwriter wrote 95% of the book. The
president just gave him the ideas.
This is the same thing here. I just worked with a company that just
happens to be also in the weight loss industry, and they had a guy
voice as a different name, as a different guy entirely. He’s a fictitious
character. He is just a representation of the guy who created the
system. And the guy who created the system didn’t want to use
himself because he’s from the Middle East and he thought that would
come across as being something Americans wouldn’t like and all this
kind of stuff. And they ended up with animated video sales letter that
worked with combined animation and video sales letter technology,
and it worked out real well.
So yeah, there’s no problems with that at all. It’s like hiring an actor to
play a part. No one’s going to criticize Leonardo DiCaprio for playing
FDR. Or who did he play?

6
like it would be an ideal place to put it, but you haven’t entered rapport.
You have in one way, okay. You’ve gotten through the nightmare
story, you’re into a state of rapport, but you’re not deep enough into the
rapport. You haven’t told them enough information.
In other words, when you get into the big problem and the bigger
solution, the big problem is where you’re going to tell them so much
information. And then when you start hinting that you have the
answer, a specific answer that goes beyond your system, that puts
your system into a blueprint that they can follow step by step, then you
can start leveling in the testimonials.
That’s the only place that you really should put them, other than after
the close, after the pitch. So, I would avoid putting them there.
Josh: 	Okay, he says, “Thanks so much.” Alright, Glenn has a question now.
“How can your VSL be used to promote a webinar?”
Jon: 	You can do a really short version of a VSL to promote a webinar.
Again, most webinars, I’ll just give you a tip here for opt-ins, most
webinars are built around how strong the opt-in page is.
So, if you just want to get people to attend your webinar, the strongest
thing you can do is just build a simple opt-in page.
We have a product called 3X Conversion Formula, that teaches people
how to build opt-in pages and emails and things that convert higher.
That’s a completely different product. But I can tell you right now that
videos are difficult to beat conversions of ugly opt-in pages.
Your goal in a webinar is to get the person to opt-in. I would opt for
that before I would opt for a video sales letter.
Where I would opt for the video sales letter in a webinar is huge. First
of all, I’d play the video sales letter first, and then give them the choice
to go with the webinar later. Because chances are if they don’t watch
the video sales letter, it’s probably due to lack of time.
So, giving them a webinar after the fact, after they exit out, is an
excellent idea. Say, “Hey, sign up for this. We got a special webinar
coming up this Tuesday. Make plan for it.” That’s the ideal place.
That’s the ideal way to use a video sales letter with a webinar. Of
course, we’re going to cover how these techniques work for a webinar.
And if you’ve written them, you kind of can see how they would work.
There’s just one difference, one key difference.
I hope that answers your question.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

document, so you can read it over.
But, Keynote allows you to just select everything, paste, and it
automatically creates slides. PowerPoint does not, because
PowerPoint’s kind of goofy that way.

Jon: 	First and foremost, and we’re going to cover exactly where to put
things, if you have a lot of traffic, it should not go on your website. It
should go on a hosted website that’s hosted with preferably Rackspace
or Amazon S3, because you will pay far less money if you have a lot of
traffic. If you don’t have a lot of traffic, you can put it on your website,
of course. So, you would send it to MyWebsite.com/Presentation, or
/Benefit. Like /WeightLoss, and send them there.
Through Facebook you can do a couple things. First of all, I just post
on my wall, “Got a great new presentation,” whatever the topic is. “Go
here,” link. If you want to put it in Facebook, you’ll have to have, I
think, a fan page to do that. I’m not sure. I’m not a Facebook expert.
But I don’t think you can just pop the video in there. You may be able
to. But it’s also going to be 50 meg, so I don’t know what Facebook’s
limitation on video is.
As far as other places to put it, the primary thing to do in other places
like Twitter, Facebook, any social media, is to drive traffic to that. You
can buy traffic for Facebook, depending on your product, or you can
just do a lot of tweets and a lot of blog posts about it. “I’ve got this new

Josh: 	Michelle says, “Would you use the same nightmare story for all of his
products, for every VSL?”
Jon: 	It depends on how diverse the products are. For example, let me give
you a quick example. For me, I’ve got 7 or 8 fitness books, right? I
lost count. The nightmare story, I have 2, but either would work for any
product, except for one. Except for one.
I have a motivational audio series called M-Power. So the nightmare
story doesn’t have anything to do with that. So if I was going to build a
video sales letter for M-Power, which right now it’s just an upsell, so
there is no need for a video sales letter, but if I was to write one for it,
this would be very simple.
I would write a nightmare story based around the fact that. “I remember
going through a period of my life where I was far more depressed than
I ever was happy, and far more motivated to sit on the couch, eat
pizza, and watch Star Trek reruns than I was to build multimillionaire
dollar companies, and climb mountains,” or whatever else I do.
So, that’s how I would start the story. In other words, I’m dealing with
motivation, so my nightmare has to be at a time in my life where I
wasn’t so motivated.
Josh: 	Okay, so here’s a question from Jonas. Jonas says he knows this

Jon: 	Let me make sure I understand the question. You mean in the video

So, if you have, in your grand offer, for example, “Part one of this 5
part book series is blank. And in this blank, you’re going to learn
blank, blank, blank.” Then yes, absolutely, you’d go through each one.
Absolutely. You want to. You definitely want to.
Now, there’s a strategy for this that’s an advanced strategy, and you
cover the main benefit points. And then after the pitch is given and the
Q&A starts, you can ask the first question. The first question is
usually, “Is there a guarantee?” Yes there is. The second question
can be something to the effect of, “Do I only get this one book?” No,
I’m giving you all these additional books too. This book, benefits. This
book, benefits. This book, benefits.
And then anyone that’s watching until the very end, the Q&A session
has been shown to be a very high-converting click section of a VSL.

Josh: 	Okay. Next is from Richard. Richard’s asking, “What if I don’t have a

Jon: 	Lie. No, I’m kidding. You don’t have results for the specific system
that you’re creating? Let me ask you this: do you have results for the
expertise of the field that you’re in?
Josh: 	Oh, he says, “Yes.”
Jon: 	There you go. Chances are very great that the system that you’re
creating is related to what you’ve already done with other people.
So, let’s just pretend for example, that you’re a counselor, and you’ve
counseled people for decades, and you’re really good at getting people
off drugs, and you’re making a system that says how to get off drugs.
That system itself, the book itself, hasn’t had any results because it
hasn’t been written yet, or isn’t finished yet, or has been read by 10
people or whatever. But you can say, “This system in this book has
been based on 20 years of successful practice in getting over 4,000
people off.” Well, there you go. Presto chango.
And when all else fails, let’s say that you don’t have a product at all,
and you’ve never even done the stuff that you’re going to do. You
woke up one day and you said, “You know what, I’m going to create a
lotion that I rub on my feet that helps me barefoot ski better.” And
Lane, at this point, if he’s still on, is laughing his ass off. Dean
Jackson, if he’s around is laughing too, because this was a real
product.
“But I don’t ski, I’ve never skied, I don’t even like skiing, and I don’t
even have a business.” Well, first of all, I’d be asking, “What are you
thinking? I’m not really sure why you would want to do that.”
But, let’s say the guy was really motivated to do this. Then the only
story that he could tell would be his own story. Say, “I hated skiing
until I created this stuff. I put it on my feet and you wouldn’t believe
what it did.” Well, that would be the only testimonial you could use
would be the one for yourself.
By the way, just so you know, we covered this in another video,
sometimes testimonials that are just about yourself are good. Because
you escape any sort of FTC regulation stuff. You escape any sort of
frowning by Google or Facebook on, “Oh, you can’t back up these
glowing testimonies.”
Okay, so let’s go on to the last few questions, buddy.

okay, well here’s the template. I got the template. I downloaded the

immense amount of work and, let me just be frank with you, an
immense amount of talent.
